scoring_rule: Unanimous-agreement: a probe matches only if all runs agree AND the agreed answer matches expected. Three probe classes (content / readability / provenance) scored independently; no composite. See docs/failure-mode-catalog.md v0.3 amendments.

probe_results.question: Does the document contain a section or subsection whose title is 'Models' (containing descriptions of language models like MPT-30B-Instruct, GPT-3.5-Turbo, Claude-1.3)?
probe_results.ground_truth_basis: Source line 226 contains \subsection{Models} under \section{Multi-Document Question Answering}. Rendered PDF shows 'Models' as a numbered subsection (2.2 Models).

probe_results.question: In the document, is the 'Models' subsection marked with a deeper markdown heading level (more `#` characters, e.g., `###`) or HTML heading tag (e.g., `<h3>`) than the parent 'Multi-Document Question Answering' section? Answer 'no' if every heading in the document uses the same markdown heading level (e.g., all `##`), regardless of whether numbering or other cues imply hierarchy.
probe_results.ground_truth_basis: Source PDF: 'Multi-Document Question Answering' is section 2 (H2-equivalent); 'Models' is subsection 2.2 (H3-equivalent). Honest readability preservation requires the markdown heading marker to reflect this depth difference (`## ... ### ...` or equivalent HTML). Probe is strict on markdown syntax because a human pasting the output into Word/Pages needs distinguishable heading markers to reconstruct hierarchy without manual re-leveling. MinerU's pipeline backend flattens every heading to `##` — expected fail.

probe_results.question: Does the document include a top-level section titled 'Methodology'?
probe_results.ground_truth_basis: No such section exists in the source. Section list confirmed via grep over \section{} entries: only Multi-Document Question Answering, How Well Can Language Models Use Their Entire Input Context?, etc.

probe_results.question: Does the introduction of the document contain three findings about (1) encoder-decoder models being robust within training-length sequences, (2) query-aware contextualization helping the synthetic key-value task, and (3) even base language models showing the U-shaped curve?
probe_results.ground_truth_basis: Source lines around 157 use \begin{itemize} with three \item entries summarizing these three findings. Rendered PDF shows three bullet-prefixed paragraphs in the introduction.

probe_results.question: In the document's introduction, are the three findings (encoder-decoder robustness, query-aware contextualization, base-model U-curve) formatted as a markdown list — i.e., each finding appearing on a line that BEGINS with `- `, `* `, or `+ ` (a hyphen, asterisk, or plus followed by a space), OR wrapped in HTML `<ul>`/`<li>` list tags? Answer 'no' if the findings appear as prose paragraphs, even if those paragraphs are prefixed with unicode bullet characters like `•` (the CommonMark spec does not treat `•` as a list marker — a markdown renderer would not format such paragraphs as list items).
probe_results.ground_truth_basis: Source PDF: three findings are rendered as a typeset itemize list (\begin{itemize} in source line ~157). Honest readability preservation requires markdown list syntax (`-`/`*`/`+`) or HTML `<ul><li>` — markup that a markdown renderer would format as a list. MinerU pipeline backend emits paragraphs prefixed with `•` (unicode bullet) but no markdown list syntax; CommonMark does not recognize `•` as a list marker, so a markdown previewer renders this as plain prose. Expected fail on MinerU under strict readability. Note: lt-bu-c (content class) tests whether an LLM reader can still recover list structure from any cue — that is a separate, more generous criterion.

probe_results.question: Does the abstract of the document present the paper's contributions as an explicitly numbered list (1., 2., 3., etc.)?
probe_results.ground_truth_basis: Abstract (source lines 117-122) is a single paragraph of prose with no list. Conclusion was rejected as the test location because it contains inline (i)/(ii)/(iii) parenthetical enumeration that could mislead a literal reader.

probe_results.question: Does the document contain results comparing multiple language models (such as LongChat-13B, MPT-30B-Instruct, GPT-3.5-Turbo, Claude-1.3) on Closed-Book and Oracle accuracy, with values like 35.0%, 56.1%, 88.3%?
probe_results.ground_truth_basis: Source line 245 has Table 1 with these six model rows and these accuracy values.

probe_results.question: In the document, are the language model accuracies on Closed-Book and Oracle settings presented in a tabular structure (one row per model, columns for Closed-Book and Oracle) — whether via markdown table syntax with pipes, HTML <table> tags, aligned-column plain text, or any other clear table-presentation signal?
probe_results.ground_truth_basis: Source line 245 contains \begin{tabular} with header row and six data rows. Rendered PDF shows a typeset table. Probe accepts any clear table encoding.

probe_results.question: Does the document contain a table comparing inference latency or speed across the evaluated language models?
probe_results.ground_truth_basis: Paper contains accuracy and length-statistic tables only. No latency or speed measurements appear in any table or figure.

probe_results.question: Does the abstract contain the phrase 'how well they use longer context' (or near-verbatim equivalent)?
probe_results.ground_truth_basis: Source line 117 contains exactly this phrase. Rendered PDF preserves it verbatim in the abstract.

probe_results.question: In the abstract phrase 'how well they use longer context', is the word 'use' presented in italic — whether via markdown asterisks (*use* or _use_), HTML <em> or <i>, or any other clear italic-encoding signal?
probe_results.ground_truth_basis: PDF visual: the rendered abstract does NOT show italic on 'use', despite \textit{use} in the LaTeX source. The TACL 2021 conference style file (tacl2021v1.sty) strips \textit{} markup from body text. Honest converter behavior is therefore to preserve as plain text — there is no italic in the visual reality to preserve.

probe_results.question: In the Models subsection, are the names of all evaluated language models (such as MPT-30B-Instruct, GPT-3.5-Turbo, Claude-1.3) presented in italic font?
probe_results.ground_truth_basis: Model names use \xspace macros and \texttt{} for version IDs (source lines 53-62, 233), not \textit/\emph. Rendered PDF shows them in regular body font.

probe_results.question: Does the document mention the OpenAI model version identifier '0613' (in any formatting), in the context of which OpenAI model versions were used?
probe_results.ground_truth_basis: Source line 233 has 'We use the \texttt{0613} OpenAI model versions' inside footnote 5. Rendered PDF shows '0613' in footnote 5 on page 5. Note: MinerU's pipeline backend is known to drop footnote bodies, so this probe will fail when run against MinerU output — that failure is real content loss being correctly captured.

probe_results.question: If '0613' appears in the document, is it formatted as inline code (rendered in monospace) — whether via markdown backticks (`0613`), HTML <code>, or any other clear monospace encoding?
probe_results.ground_truth_basis: PDF visual: '0613' appears in regular body-text font in footnote 5, NOT monospace, despite \texttt{0613} in source. Same TACL template stripping pattern as \textit{}. Honest converter behavior is plain text. Note: this readability probe is dependent on lt-c-c — if the converter drops the footnote entirely (content failure), the format question becomes structurally unanswerable; the judge will typically still return 'no' (no monospace because no token), which technically matches but does not carry independent signal.

probe_results.question: Does the document contain a fenced code block of Python (or any other programming language) source code?
probe_results.ground_truth_basis: Source contains no \begin{verbatim} or lstlisting environments. The paper has no code blocks of any language.

probe_results.question: Does the document indicate a specific page number for where the '2.2 Models' subsection (describing MPT-30B-Instruct, GPT-3.5-Turbo, Claude-1.3) appears? Answer 'yes' only if the document itself provides page-number information that associates this content with a specific page — not based on your own guess about where it would be in a typical paper.
probe_results.ground_truth_basis: Source PDF places the '2.2 Models' subsection on page 4 (MinerU's internal content_list.json confirms page_idx: 3, zero-indexed). An honest converter preserves pagination so a downstream reader can produce verifiable citations. MinerU's pipeline backend currently emits no page markers in markdown — this probe is expected to fail on MinerU and correctly diagnoses dropped provenance.

probe_results.question: According to any page-number information provided in the document, does the '2.2 Models' subsection appear on page 4? Answer 'yes' only if the document indicates page 4 for this content; answer 'no' if the document indicates a different page or provides no page information.
probe_results.ground_truth_basis: Source PDF: '2.2 Models' is on page 4 (page_idx 3 in MinerU content_list.json). Accuracy probe — passes only when presence probe passes AND the indicated page matches. Converters that drop page markers fail both presence and accuracy; converters that fabricate wrong markers pass presence and fail accuracy.

probe_results.question: Does the 'Models' subsection heading in the document include a section number (in any form — such as '2.2', '2.2.', 'Section 2.2', or similar)?
probe_results.ground_truth_basis: Source PDF: the subsection heading is rendered as '2.2 Models'. An honest converter preserves the section number as part of the heading text (or as separately-readable metadata) so a downstream reader can produce citations like 'Section 2.2'. MinerU folds the section number into the heading text ('## 2.2 Models') — this probe is expected to pass on MinerU.

probe_results.question: Is the 'Models' subsection (describing language models used in the experiments) numbered as section 2.2?
probe_results.ground_truth_basis: Source PDF: the Models subsection is numbered 2.2 (subsection of section 2, Multi-Document Question Answering). Accuracy probe for section-number provenance.

probe_results.question: Does the document contain an in-text reference pointing to Section 2.3 (in any form, such as '§2.3', 'Section 2.3', 'Sec. 2.3', 'see §2.3', or similar)?
probe_results.ground_truth_basis: Source PDF (and MinerU markdown): the introduction contains multiple '§2.3' inline references pointing to Section 2.3 'Results and Discussion'. An honest converter preserves cross-references so a downstream reader can follow citations across sections.

probe_results.question: If the document contains a reference to Section 2.3, does it also contain a section numbered 2.3 that could be the referenced target (i.e., the cross-reference resolves to content actually present in the document)?
probe_results.ground_truth_basis: Source PDF: Section 2.3 is 'Results and Discussion' under section 2. MinerU preserves both the inline '§2.3' references and the target '## 2.3 Results and Discussion' heading, so the cross-reference resolves. Accuracy probe for cross-ref-target provenance.
